conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

Let me first review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. To help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the first one:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children. I'm asking about both of them – Alex, who's 6, and Blake, who's 3.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have two children - Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 3).

Now I need to ask you: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I live in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

**Do both Alex and Blake currently live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

Just one more question: **Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital or care for extended periods, or are there any other special circumstances regarding their living arrangements that I should know about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex is just at home with me as normal. But Blake has actually been in hospital for a while now – a few months. He's had some health issues that have needed treatment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Blake has been unwell. I need to understand this situation better to assess eligibility correctly.

**How many weeks has Blake been in hospital continuously?** (Or approximately how many months, and I can work it out?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about 13 weeks now, so just over three months.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

One more important question about Blake's situation: **Do you regularly spend money on Blake while he's in hospital - for example, on things like clothes, toys, treats, or other items for him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Honestly, no, I haven't really been able to afford to spend money on him while he's been in there. It's been a difficult time financially.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, and thank you for being honest with me about your circumstances. I have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary of Your Child Benefit Eligibility

**For Alex (age 6): You ARE eligible ✓**

Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, you're responsible for them, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You meet all the standard eligibility requirements for Alex.

**For Blake (age 3): You are NOT eligible ✗**

Unfortunately, because Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks (which exceeds the 12-week threshold), and you haven't been regularly spending money on them during this hospital stay, you would not be eligible to continue receiving Child Benefit for Blake beyond the 12-week period.

**Important notes:**
- You would have been entitled to Child Benefit for Blake for the first 12 weeks of their hospital stay
- If your circumstances change and Blake comes home from hospital, you would be able to claim Child Benefit for them again
- If you are able to start regularly spending money on Blake while they're in hospital, this could affect your eligibility - you may want to contact HMRC to discuss this

I hope Blake recovers soon and is able to come home. If you have any questions about this assessment, please let me know.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), lives with you in the UK, and you are responsible for them. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and there are no special circumstances affecting eligibility. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16) and normally lives with you in the UK. However, Blake has been in hospital continuously for 13 weeks (just over 3 months). According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. After the 12-week limit, you are usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless there are exceptions - such as if you regularly spend money on the child while they are in hospital. You have confirmed that you have not been able to regularly spend money on Blake during this time. Therefore, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake after the 12-week period has passed.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). They meet the standard eligibility criteria for Alex. However, Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks continuously, which exceeds the 12-week threshold. After 12 weeks in hospital, Child Benefit eligibility ceases unless the claimant regularly spends money on the child. Since the claimant has not been able to afford to regularly spend money on Blake during the hospital stay, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake beyond the 12-week period.

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and there are no special circumstances affecting eligibility. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16) and normally lives with you in the UK. However, Blake has been in hospital continuously for 13 weeks (just over 3 months). According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. After the 12-week limit, you are usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless there are exceptions - such as if you regularly spend money on the child while they are in hospital. You have confirmed that you have not been able to regularly spend money on Blake during this time. Therefore, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake after the 12-week period has passed.

After 12 weeks in hospital, Child Benefit eligibility ceases unless the claimant regularly spends money on the child. Since the claimant has not been able to afford to regularly spend money on Blake during the hospital stay, they are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake beyond the 12-week period.
